ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried beef with onions; mapped to broader 'beef dish' class as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for stir-fried beef with onions; A0FJP (Beef, cooked) used as the closest defensible broader anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for stir-fried beef with onions; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried beef with onions is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a standard side dish portion served alongside steamed rice in a Singaporean meal.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Beef with Onions — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Stir Fried Beef with Onions' (cr_id F06040019). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried beef with onions. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with only 2.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g. GI estimated at approximately 15 (low) by analogy with other high-protein, low-carbohydrate beef dishes and mixed meat-vegetable stir-fries documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 4.35 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.7, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 494 mg per 100 g, substantially exceeding the 400 mg per 100 g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods, driven by soy sauce and oyster sauce in the marinade.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g and request preparation with reduced-sodium soy sauce and minimal oyster sauce to bring sodium closer to 250–300 mg per 100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is low at 2.9 g per 100 g with minimal sugar at 0.7 g per 100 g; high protein at 17.65 g per 100 g slows gastric emptying and supports postprandial blood glucose stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions without added sugar or sweet sauces; monitor total meal carbohydrate load from accompanying rice or noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 494 mg per 100 g poses a cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; saturated fat from beef (estimated 1.8 g per 100 g) contributes to LDL cholesterol load when consumed frequently.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request low-sodium preparation, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to offset the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory pathways directly relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; sodium at 494 mg per 100 g also exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold, worsening tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish; consider substituting beef with chicken breast, turkey, or oily fish to reduce arachidonic acid intake and inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried beef with onions is a popular Singaporean Chinese home-cooked and hawker dish consisting of thinly sliced beef marinated in soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and ginger, then wok-fried at high heat with sliced onions until the beef is just cooked and the onions are softened and lightly caramelised. The dish delivers a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with a tender, slightly chewy beef texture and a mild sweetness from the onions. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed white rice or fried rice in a Singaporean meal context. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (17.65 g per 100 g) and relatively low in carbohydrates (2.9 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Fat content is moderate at 4.75 g per 100 g. The primary clinical concern is sodium at 494 mg per 100 g, which is elevated due to soy sauce and oyster sauce, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis without significant sodium reduction. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker and restaurant contexts. Glycemic impact is negligible given the very low carbohydrate content.
common_user_queries: Is stir-fried beef with onions healthy?
How much protein is in stir-fried beef with onions?
Is stir-fried beef with onions suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat beef stir fry if I have high blood pressure?
Is stir-fried beef with onions halal?
How many calories in stir-fried beef with onions?
What is the sodium content of stir-fried beef with onions?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06040019) with a confidence of 0.95. The dish is a canonical Singaporean Chinese preparation with well-documented nutritional composition. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef composition data as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; this is the only adapted value.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific stir-fried beef with onions preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F06040019) as of 2026. saturated_fat_g (1.8 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked beef (lean, pan-fried) as the closest approved-source analogue, given HPB does not publish saturated fat breakdown for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 15, low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database for high-protein, low-carbohydrate beef and mixed meat-vegetable stir-fries; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. The dietary tag 'low_calorie' from the input is retained as contextually reasonable (125 kcal per 100 g) but should be reviewed against Singapore HPB low-calorie labelling thresholds.
